med_suggestions.notes: Common first-line for uncomplicated cystitis when renal function adequate and no systemic signs.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Avoid if pyelonephritis suspected (fever, flank pain); avoid if CrCl < 30 mL/min; nitrofurantoin allergy; caution near term pregnancy.

med_suggestions.notes: Pregnancy-compatible option; consider if nitrofurantoin not appropriate.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Cephalosporin allergy; use caution with immediate-type severe beta-lactam allergy; adjust if renal impairment.

med_suggestions.exclusions: Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; adjust for renal impairment.

med_suggestions.notes: Single-dose alternative; generally pregnancy-compatible but confirm clinician judgment and local guidance.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Not for suspected pyelonephritis; consider availability/cost.

med_suggestions.dosing: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.sig: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.notes: Alternative first-line when pregnancy status allows and resistance risk acceptable.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Sulfa allergy; avoid 1st trimester if possible (folate antagonist) and near term (kernicterus risk); consider local resistance; drug interactions.

note.soap:
S:  
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 3-day history of dysuria described as burning during urination. She reports associated fever but denies flank pain or vaginal discharge. No medication allergies were noted.

O:  
Phone history only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A:  
- Working diagnosis (most likely):  
  Uncomplicated urinary tract infection (cystitis) with associated fever.  
- Differential:  
  1. Pyelonephritis (less likely given no flank pain reported)  
  2. Vaginitis or sexually transmitted infection (no discharge reported)  
  3. Urethritis  
  4. Interstitial cystitis (less likely with fever present)  
- Red flags considered:  
  Fever present, which may indicate upper urinary tract involvement; no flank pain reported. No signs of severe systemic illness or sepsis reported.

P:  
- Disposition:  
  Patient can be managed initially with oral antibiotics; if symptoms worsen or flank pain develops, further evaluation is warranted.  
- Plan: Nitrofurantoin (macrocrystals/monohydrate) — 100 mg PO BID x 5 days.  Alternative: Cephalexin (500 mg PO QID x 5–7 days).
  Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
  - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
  - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
  - Nitrofurantoin (macrocrystals/monohydrate) — 100 mg PO BID x 5 days
    - Notes: Common first-line for uncomplicated cystitis when renal function adequate and no systemic signs.
    - Exclusions: Avoid if pyelonephritis suspected (fever, flank pain); avoid if CrCl < 30 mL/min; nitrofurantoin allergy; caution near term pregnancy.
  - Cephalexin — 500 mg PO QID x 5–7 days
    - Notes: Pregnancy-compatible option; consider if nitrofurantoin not appropriate.
    - Exclusions: Cephalosporin allergy; use caution with immediate-type severe beta-lactam allergy; adjust if renal impairment.
  - Amoxicillin-clavulanate — 875/125 mg PO BID x 5–7 days
    - Notes: Reasonable alternative, especially if pregnancy or other constraints; confirm indication/local resistance.
    - Exclusions: Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; adjust for renal impairment.
  - Fosfomycin tromethamine — 3 g PO once
    - Notes: Single-dose alternative; generally pregnancy-compatible but confirm clinician judgment and local guidance.
    - Exclusions: Not for suspected pyelonephritis; consider availability/cost.
  - Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) — 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
    - Notes: Alternative first-line when pregnancy status allows and resistance risk acceptable.
    - Exclusions: Sulfa allergy; avoid 1st trimester if possible (folate antagonist) and near term (kernicterus risk); consider local resistance; drug interactions.
  - Supportive care:
  - Hydration: Encourage fluids as tolerated.
  - Pain control: Acetaminophen or ibuprofen if safe for patient (avoid NSAIDs in late pregnancy; avoid if renal disease, ulcer/GI bleed risk).
  - Follow-up and safety:
  - Expected course: Should start improving within 24–48 hours after antibiotics.
  - Re-evaluation: If not improving within 48–72 hours, reassess diagnosis/resistance and consider urine culture and/or in-person evaluation.
  - Urgent/ED: Seek urgent care/ED for flank pain, persistent high fever/chills, vomiting/dehydration, pregnancy with systemic symptoms, inability to urinate, or worsening despite treatment.
  Empiric antibiotic treatment for urinary tract infection is appropriate. Given the presence of fever, consider treatment covering possible pyelonephritis. Typical regimens include:  
  - Nitrofurantoin 100 mg orally twice daily for 5 days (if no suspicion of pyelonephritis)  
  - Alternatively, if pyelonephritis suspected or fever persists, a fluoroquinolone such as ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days may be considered, pending local resistance patterns and pregnancy status.  
  Note: Pregnancy status was reported but not to be mentioned; treatment choice should consider pregnancy if known.  
  Recommended regimen; reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.  
- Safety net:  
  Advise patient to seek urgent care if she develops worsening fever, flank pain, inability to tolerate oral intake, vomiting, or signs of systemic illness. If symptoms do not improve within 48-72 hours of treatment initiation, follow-up evaluation is recommended.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.

evidence.title: IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
